unitTitle: Y8 Introduction to Python programming (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: First steps — algorithms, programs and your first Python code
lessons.objectives:
I can explain the difference between an algorithm and a program
I can write a Python program that prints a message
I can use a variable and input to greet the user
I can find and fix a common syntax error
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: algorithm · program · variable · input · output · syntax error. TA: everyone has the Python editor open (Mu or Trinket); pupils work in pairs (driver/navigator, swap each task). Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — algorithm or program? Pupils DRAG cards (card-sort) on the starter worksheet: steps for a person vs code for a computer. No writing for Support.
   • Support: tick what an algorithm is.  • Core: write one difference.  • Challenge: why a program needs exact rules.
2. (8–18) I-do — live-code “Hello world”. Type it slowly; point out brackets and quotation marks; make one deliberate mistake (capital P) and read the error together. Link Scratch ask/answer → Python input().
3. (18–38) You-do (pair programming) — pupils write a program that asks a name and says hello. Activity worksheet: Support fill-the-gaps (print/=/input) + card-sort output/input/assignment; Core write the greeting + “what does = do”; Challenge tick-all-mistakes (multi-select) + rewrite the line. Likely error: missing bracket/quote → fix-words “read the error, find the line, count the brackets.”
4. (38–48) Syntax errors — normalise them: everyone gets them, fix one thing at a time. Pupils paste a link + a screenshot of their running program (Show your work).
5. (48–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pair shows their greeting program. TA notes who met all four.
Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Crunching numbers — arithmetic, assignment and numerical input
lessons.objectives:
I can use arithmetic operators to calculate a value
I can explain what an assignment does (work out the right side, then store it)
I can use int() to turn keyboard input into a number
I can put program lines in the correct order so a variable is set before it is used
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: operator · expression · assignment · integer · int(). TA: editor open; pupils in pairs (swap each task). Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — make predictions. Starter worksheet: MATCH operators (+ - * //) to meaning; predict the value of `double` (misconception trap: an assignment is not an equation).
   • Support: which way Python reads an assignment.  • Core: why `double` stays 10.  • Challenge: trace `number = number + 10`.
2. (10–20) I-do/we-do — assignment with expressions; walk-through right-to-left. PARSONS “Order matters” on the activity worksheet (a variable must be set before it is used → NameError otherwise).
3. (20–28) I-do — live-code the age/weight program; run WITHOUT int() to show the TypeError, then add int() and run again. Fix-words: “input gives text — wrap it in int() to do maths.”
4. (28–48) You-do (pairs) — Support fill-the-gap int(input()); Core weight-on-the-moon; Challenge dog-years + explain int(). Pupils paste a link + screenshot (Show your work).
5. (48–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; share one calculation. TA notes who used int() correctly. Homework available (calculations + a rearrange task).
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: At a crossroads — selection (if/else) and randomness
lessons.objectives:
I can use a comparison operator (==, !=, <, >) to make a condition
I can use if and else to choose between two paths
I can generate a random integer with randint()
I can build a simple number guessing game
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: selection · condition · comparison operator · if · else · random. TA: editor open; pairs (swap each task). Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — something missing. Starter worksheet: MATCH comparison operators (== != < >); a program with no `if` always does the same thing.
   • Support: what an `if` chooses.  • Core: an everyday if/else.  • Challenge: why a condition must be true/false.
2. (5–17) I-do — live-code an if/else greeting. Syntax pitfalls: lowercase if/else, the colon, `==` not `=`, indentation. Show both branches with your hands — only ONE runs.
3. (17–37) You-do (pairs) — film-critic / number-guessing game. Activity worksheet: PARSONS order the if/else (indentation!); Support fill-the-gap `==` + indentation tick; Core build the game; Challenge add randomness (randint) + explain `=` vs `==`. Likely error: `=` in a condition → fix-words “one equals stores, two equals compares.”
4. (37–47) Randomness — `from random import randint`, `randint(1, 20)`; model a temporary print to reveal the secret while testing, then remove it. Pupils paste a link + screenshot.
5. (47–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; closed questions on selection. TA notes who tested BOTH branches.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound.

resources.title: Film critic if/else code (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: More branches — multi-branch selection (if/elif/else) and meeting iteration
lessons.objectives:
I can use if/elif/else for more than two cases
I can choose the right number of branches for a problem
I can describe how a while loop repeats while its condition is true
I can predict the output of a simple program
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: multi-branch selection · elif · condition · iteration · while. TA: editor open; pairs (swap each task). Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — the weather. Starter worksheet: MATCH weather → advice (four cases); one if/else only gives two choices.
   • Support: which keyword handles many cases.  • Core: why one if/else is not enough.  • Challenge: why elif beats nesting.
2. (5–25) I-do — if/elif/else: Python checks each condition in order and runs the FIRST true one. Live-code the weather program. PARSONS order the if/elif/else on the activity worksheet (indentation marks each branch).
3. (25–37) You-do (pairs) — “people in space”: Support fill-the-gap `elif`; Core extend to too-high/too-low/correct; Challenge explain why elif beats three separate ifs. A ready-made `people()` function hides the hard part.
4. (37–42) Something missing — repeating; recall Scratch loops; introduce `while`.
5. (42–55) Iteration — ORDER the steps of one while-loop pass (check condition → run block → back to top → check again → stop when false). Walk through ONE pass slowly. Common error: thinks the condition is checked mid-loop — it is checked at the top. Pupils paste a link + screenshot.
Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”. Homework available. Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks routine.

resources.title: People in space code (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Round and round — counters in while loops
lessons.objectives:
I can use a while loop with a counter variable
I can start, change and check a counter to control a loop
I can build a countdown that stops at the right number
I can use a loop to ask several questions
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: iteration · counter · condition · while. TA: editor open; pairs (swap each task). Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — trace the counter. Starter worksheet: `count = count - 1` makes count one smaller; predict the output (3 then 2).
   • Support: what `count - 1` does.  • Core: what a counter is.  • Challenge: make it go up by 3.
2. (5–20) We-do — a countdown loop. PARSONS order the countdown (count = 10 … print “Lift off”). CARD-SORT: which lines run once (set-up / Lift off) vs every round (print / change the counter). Indentation shows what is inside the loop.
3. (20–25) I-do — live-code times tables: start with `while True` (forever), add a `questions` counter, change the condition to `questions < 3`; show how to stop a runaway loop (Stop / Ctrl+C).
4. (25–48) You-do (pairs) — Support choose the right condition + fill the gap; Core countdown with Lift off; Challenge 3-question game + explain where the counter changes. Likely error: forgetting to change the counter → never ends → fix-words “every loop must change the counter.” Pupils paste a link + screenshot; rate confidence (slider).
5. (48–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”. TA notes who can explain where the counter changes.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks routine.

resources.title: Times tables while-loop code (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Putting it all together — flags and the finished guessing game
lessons.objectives:
I can combine a while loop with if/else
I can use a Boolean flag (True/False) to control a loop
I can count and limit the number of guesses
I can finish and test a complete number guessing game
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: iteration · selection · Boolean · flag · and. TA: editor open; pairs (swap each task). Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–3) Starter — which parts repeat? Starter worksheet: CARD-SORT pick-the-number (once) vs ask/feedback (repeat); the secret number must be picked OUTSIDE the loop.
   • Support: what a Boolean holds.  • Core: why pick-once.  • Challenge: a flag never raised → never ends.
2. (3–10) I-do — flags: a True/False variable, like a flag up or down. Live-code adding the `while guessed == False` loop; show that a flag never raised means the game never ends, then fix it.
3. (10–30) You-do (pairs) — Lucky number revisited. Activity worksheet: MATCH True/False/flag/and; Support fill the gaps (True, False); Core raise the flag + count guesses; Challenge limit to 3 guesses with `and` + reveal the number. Likely error: flag set True every round → fix-words “only raise the flag inside the correct if.” Pupils paste a link + screenshot; rate confidence and write one takeaway (reflection slider).
4. (30–50) Summative assessment — ~20 closed questions across the whole unit (online quiz if possible for instant feedback). Separate from the worksheet; keep it calm and low-stakes.
5. (50–55) Reflection — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; one thing learnt. TA gathers takeaways.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks routine.
